section.title: Flee Idolatry, Do All to God's Glory
section.sectionRationale: Paul brings the whole discussion of idol food to its point. Because the Lord's Supper is a real participation in the blood and body of Christ, and because the one loaf makes the many one body, believers cannot also share the table of demons. From this he draws the pastoral resolution: freedom is real, but it must be governed by love that seeks the good of others and by a single aim, the glory of God. Whether eating, drinking, or anything else, do all to God's glory, giving no offense, seeking the salvation of many, imitating Paul as he imitates Christ.
section.thesis: Because the cup and the bread are a participation in the blood and body of Christ, and the one loaf makes the many one body, believers must flee idolatry and cannot share the table of demons; and since everything is to be done for the glory of God, freedom is to be ruled by love that seeks the good and salvation of many, imitating Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: flee idolatry because the Supper is a real sharing in Christ and makes one body; the impossibility of sharing the Lord's table and the table of demons; freedom governed by love that seeks the good of others; and the summit, do all to the glory of God, giving no offense and imitating Christ.

units.label: Flee idolatry, one loaf one body

verses.text: Therefore, my beloved, flee from {{eidololatria|idolatry}}.
I speak to reasonable people; judge for yourselves what I say.
Is not the cup of blessing that we bless a {{koinonia|participation}} in the blood of Christ? And is not the bread that we break a participation in the body of Christ?
Because there is one loaf, we who are many are one body; for we all partake of the one loaf.
Consider the people of Israel: Are not those who eat the sacrifices fellow partakers in the altar?
Am I suggesting, then, that food sacrificed to an idol is anything, or that an idol is anything?
No, but the sacrifices of pagans are offered to {{daimonion|demons}}, not to God. And I do not want you to be participants with demons.
You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons too; you cannot partake in the table of the Lord and the table of demons too.
Are we trying to provoke the Lord to jealousy? Are we stronger than He?
“Everything is permissible,” but not everything is beneficial. “Everything is permissible,” but not everything is edifying.
No one should seek his own good, but the good of others.
Eat anything sold in the meat market without raising questions of conscience,
for, “The earth is the Lord’s, and the fullness thereof.”
If an unbeliever invites you to a meal and you want to go, eat anything set before you without raising questions of conscience.
But if someone tells you, “This food was offered to idols,” then do not eat it, for the sake of the one who told you and for the sake of conscience,
the other one’s conscience, I mean, not your own. For why should my freedom be determined by someone else’s conscience?
If I partake in the meal with thankfulness, why am I denounced because of that for which I give thanks?
So whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do it all to the {{doxa|glory}} of God.
Do not become a stumbling block, whether to Jews or Greeks or the church of God,
as I also try to please everyone in all I do. For I am not seeking my own good, but the good of many, that they may be saved.
You are to imitate me, just as I {{mimeomai|imitate}} Christ.

eidololatria.gloss: Idolatry, the worship or service of images. Paul commands the church to flee it, not to test how near they can stand. Because the living God will share His people with no rival, idolatry is not a harmless custom but a betrayal that provokes the Lord's jealousy.

koinonia.gloss: Participation, communion, a real sharing in common. The cup and the bread are a koinonia in the blood and body of Christ, a genuine joining to Him and to one another, not a bare symbol. It is the theological engine of the passage: because the Supper truly unites us to Christ, we cannot also unite ourselves to demons at their table.

daimonion.gloss: A demon, an evil spirit. An idol is nothing, yet the sacrifices of pagans are offered to demons behind the images. To share a pagan sacrificial meal is to become a participant with demons, which is why the two tables are utterly incompatible.

doxa.gloss: Glory, weight, honor, the manifest worth of God. The whole ethic of freedom is gathered under one aim: whatever you do, do it all to the glory of God. It reframes eating, drinking, and every ordinary act as an opportunity to display and honor the worth of God.

mimeomai.gloss: To imitate, to follow as a model. Paul calls the church to imitate him only insofar as he imitates Christ, so the chain of discipleship leads always back to the Lord. The self-denying, others-seeking freedom he has described is finally the shape of Christ's own life.

basic.context: Paul finishes the long discussion about food offered to idols with a clear command and a soaring principle. The command is simple: flee idolatry. He explains why with the Lord's Supper. When the church shares the cup and the bread, it is really sharing in the blood and body of Christ, and the one loaf makes the many into one body. That kind of real sharing cannot be mixed with the pagan sacrificial meals, because behind the idols are demons. You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons. Then he answers practical questions about buying meat and eating at a neighbor's house: use your freedom, but always for the good of others and, above all, for the glory of God. Whatever you do, do it all to God's glory, seeking the salvation of many, following Paul as he follows Christ.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul distinguishes carefully. An idol is nothing, and meat is just meat, so ordinary market food may be eaten freely with thanksgiving. But deliberate participation in idol worship is spiritual treason, because it joins a person to demons and provokes God's jealousy. The governing rules are love, which seeks the good of others and refuses to be a stumbling block, and God's glory, which is the aim of every act. Verse 31 lifts a debate about food into a rule for the whole of life.
basic.originalLanguages: The section opens with a command to flee eidololatria, idolatry. Its reason is koinonia: the cup and bread are a real participation in the blood and body of Christ. Behind pagan sacrifices stand daimonion, demons, so the two tables cannot be shared. The whole matter is then gathered under one word, doxa, glory: do it all to the glory of God. And the pattern is set by mimeomai, imitation, following Paul as he follows Christ.
basic.typology: Israel's sacrificial meals, where those who ate the offerings shared in the altar, are the pattern Paul uses to explain the Lord's Supper: eating at a table joins you to what the table serves. The Passover and fellowship offerings that made worshippers partakers in the altar find their fulfillment in the Supper, where believers share in Christ, our Passover, once for all sacrificed.
basic.christ: The center of this passage is the Lord's Supper as a real sharing in the crucified and risen Christ. His shed blood and given body are the church's true food, and communion with Him is so real that it makes many people one body and rules out communion with demons. The closing verse names Him as the final model: Paul's self-giving freedom, and the church's, is simply the imitation of Christ, who sought not His own good but ours, that we might be saved.
basic.god: God is jealous for His people with a holy jealousy; to flirt with idols is to provoke the Lord, and we are not stronger than He. Yet He is also the generous Creator: the earth is the Lord's and everything in it, so His people may eat with thanksgiving. Above all He is the God whose glory is the goal of every action, the one whose worth we display when we lay down our rights to seek the good and salvation of others.
basic.crossReferences: The Gospels record Jesus giving the cup and the bread as His blood and body. Deuteronomy warns that Israel's sacrifices to demons provoke God's jealousy. The Psalms declare the earth is the Lord's and its fullness. Philippians calls the church to look to the interests of others as Christ did.
basic.oneStory: From Eden onward the great human failure has been idolatry, giving to created things the worship owed to God, and the great rescue is a people reconciled to worship Him alone. Here the church, joined to Christ at His table, is called out of idolatry into a life whose single aim is God's glory. In seeking the good and salvation of many rather than their own, believers imitate Christ and take their place in the story that ends with every knee bowing to Him.

context.literary: This unit resolves the argument running from chapter eight through chapter ten. It moves from a blunt command, flee idolatry, to its theological ground in the Lord's Supper and the one body, to the impossibility of sharing two tables, and finally to practical case studies about market meat and dinner invitations, before rising to the climactic principle of God's glory and the call to imitate Christ, which straddles the chapter break at 11:1.
context.historical: Much civic and social life in Corinth revolved around meals in temple precincts and meat that had passed through pagan sacrifice before reaching the market. Believers faced constant decisions about attending idol feasts, buying market meat, and dining with pagan neighbors. Paul threads these situations with a distinction between genuine idol worship, which is forbidden, and neutral food, which may be eaten with thanks and love.
context.canonical: The passage is one of the New Testament's fullest statements on the Lord's Supper as participation in Christ, alongside the words of institution in the Gospels and in chapter eleven. Its principle that all of life is to be lived for the glory of God gathers up the Old Testament vision of God's people bearing His name and points forward to the worship of the whole redeemed creation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To forbid participation in idol worship on the ground that the Lord's Supper is a real communion with Christ that cannot be mixed with communion with demons, and to resolve the disputed questions of freedom by two controlling principles, love that seeks the good of others and the overarching glory of God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific rulings about market meat and dinner invitations are wise applications to Corinth's situation, showing how the principles work. What is prescriptive across all times is the command to flee idolatry, the truth that the Supper is a real participation in Christ, and the aims that govern all conduct: the good of others and the glory of God.

debates.question: How real is the participation in Christ at the Lord's Supper, verse 16?

views.label: A genuine spiritual communion
views.case: Paul's word koinonia and his parallel with Israel's altar and the pagan tables show a real sharing, by the Spirit, in the crucified and risen Christ, not an empty symbol; the reality is what makes the two tables incompatible.

views.label: A covenant sign and pledge
views.case: Others emphasize that the bread and cup are signs that seal the believer's union with Christ and the church, so the sharing is real yet received by faith, without a change in the elements themselves.

debates.bearing: Both orthodox readings affirm a true communion with Christ at His table that binds believers to Him and to one another and forbids any parallel communion with idols.

study.originalLanguages: The passage opens with an urgent command: flee eidololatria, idolatry. Its logic rests on koinonia, participation: the cup we bless and the bread we break are a real sharing in the blood and body of Christ, and that one loaf makes the many one body. Behind the idols stand daimonion, demons, so no one can share the Lord's table and the table of demons at once. The disputed questions of freedom are then settled under one word, doxa: whatever you do, do it all to the glory of God. And the final pattern is mimeomai, imitation, the church following Paul as he follows the self-giving Christ.

typology.type: Eating the sacrifices, sharing in the altar
typology.fulfillment: Israel's sacrificial meals, which made worshippers partakers in the altar, are fulfilled in the Lord's Supper, where believers truly share in Christ, our Passover, once for all sacrificed.
The single loaf shared by many pictures the one body of Christ, the church united in Him, the goal toward which God's covenant people always pointed.

study.christ: Christ crucified and risen is the heart of this passage. His blood and body, given at the cross, are shared at His table, and that sharing is so real that it unites the many into one body and cannot coexist with communion with demons. He is the provision behind the thankful meal, since the earth and its fullness are the Lord's. And He is the final model of the whole ethic: the closing verse calls the church to imitate Paul only as Paul imitates Christ, the one who sought not His own good but the good of the many, that they might be saved.
study.god: God is revealed as jealous and glorious and generous. He will not share His people's worship with idols, and to try provokes His jealousy, for we are not stronger than He. Yet He is the Creator who owns the earth and its fullness, so His people eat freely and give thanks. Supremely He is the God whose glory is the point of everything, the aim that turns eating and drinking and every ordinary act into worship and that frees His people to seek the salvation of others.

crossReferences.note: Jesus gives the bread and cup as His body and blood, the institution behind the participation of verse 16.
Israel sacrificed to demons and provoked God to jealousy, the background to verses 20 to 22.
The earth is the Lord's and the fullness thereof, quoted in verse 26 to ground freedom to eat with thanks.
Look to the interests of others, having the mind of Christ, echoing seek the good of many and imitate Christ.
You are not your own; glorify God with your body, kindred to do all to the glory of God.

study.oneStory: The Bible's long war is against idolatry and for a people who worship God alone, and its resolution is a church joined to Christ. At His table believers share in His body and blood and are made one body, called out of every rival communion. Freed from seeking their own good, they live for the glory of God and the salvation of many, imitating Christ. This is the shape of the redeemed life, a foretaste of the day when all creation will do everything to the glory of God.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say believers cannot share the table of the Lord and the table of demons?

options.text: Because the Supper is a real participation in Christ that cannot be mixed with communion with demons.
options.note: Verses 16 to 21: real sharing in Christ rules out sharing with demons behind the idols.

options.text: Because sharing the pagan table makes one a participant with demons.
options.note: True and stated in verse 20, though the deeper reason is the incompatible communion with Christ at His table.

options.text: Because idols are powerful gods to be feared.
options.note: An idol is nothing, yet demons stand behind pagan sacrifice.

options.text: Because eating any meat is forbidden.
options.note: Paul frees them to eat market meat with thanks; the issue is idol worship.

quiz.q: What does koinonia mean in the phrase a participation in the blood of Christ?

options.text: A real sharing and communion in Christ, not a bare symbol.
options.note: The reality of the sharing is what makes the two tables incompatible.

options.text: A covenant sign that seals our union with Christ, received by faith.
options.note: A faithful reading that still affirms real communion, though it emphasizes the sign rather than the sharing itself.

options.text: A mere reminder with no real connection to Christ.
options.note: Paul's argument depends on a genuine participation.

options.text: A private feeling unrelated to the church.
options.note: The one loaf makes the many one body; it is deeply corporate.

quiz.q: How should believers use their freedom, according to this passage?

options.text: For the good of others and the glory of God, giving no offense.
options.note: Verses 24, 31, and 32 govern freedom by love and God's glory.

options.text: Freely, but only in ways that are beneficial and build others up.
options.note: True to verse 23, though the fullest answer names God's glory and the good of others as the aim.

options.text: However they please, since everything is permissible.
options.note: Permissible, yes, but not everything is beneficial or edifying.

options.text: By avoiding all contact with unbelievers.
options.note: Paul allows dining with unbelievers; the aim is their good and salvation.

quiz.q: What is the overarching principle of verse 31?

options.text: Whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do it all to the glory of God.
options.note: The whole of life, not just food, is to be lived for God's glory.

options.text: Even ordinary acts like eating and drinking can honor God.
options.note: True and drawn from the verse, though the principle sweeps wider to whatever you do.

options.text: Only religious acts count as glorifying God.
options.note: Even eating and drinking are to glorify God, so the principle covers all of life.

options.text: Avoid food and drink to be holy.
options.note: The point is not abstinence but doing all to God's glory.

quiz.q: Whom does Paul finally point to as the model to imitate?

options.text: Christ, whom Paul himself imitates in seeking the good of many.
options.note: 11:1: imitate me as I imitate Christ; the chain leads back to the Lord.

options.text: Paul, but only as he follows Christ's example.
options.note: Right that Paul is a pattern, though the final model he points to is Christ Himself.

options.text: Paul alone, as the ultimate example.
options.note: Paul is a model only insofar as he imitates Christ.

options.text: The surrounding pagan culture.
options.note: He calls them away from idolatry, not toward the culture.

questions: Paul says flee idolatry, not merely avoid it. What in your life competes with God for your worship, and what would fleeing rather than flirting with it look like?
The Lord's Supper is a real sharing in Christ and unites us as one body. How might a deeper sense of that reality change the way you come to the Lord's table and to your church family?
Everything may be permissible, but not everything is beneficial or edifying. Where do you need to weigh your freedom by love for others rather than by your rights?
Verse 31 makes God's glory the aim of eating, drinking, and everything. Take one ordinary part of your day and ask how it could be done to the glory of God.
Paul sought the good of many that they might be saved, imitating Christ. Whose salvation might God be calling you to seek by laying down some comfort or preference?

translationNotes.note: Participation translates koinonia, a real communion or sharing; the cup and bread genuinely join believers to Christ and to one another, which is why the passage rules out any parallel sharing with demons.
An idol is nothing, yet Paul says pagan sacrifices are offered to demons; the images are empty, but real spiritual powers stand behind idol worship, making it far from harmless.
This verse belongs with the preceding movement, not the next; it seals the argument by pointing beyond Paul to Christ, whom he imitates in seeking the good of many.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 10:14 to 11:1. Any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged.
